name: An Afternoon of Bach

location.name: Mazzoleni Concert Hall, Royal Conservatory of Music

description: Featuring former Toronto Symphony Orchestra's Principal Trumpet Andrew McCandless, alongside faculty and students of The Glenn Gould School, this program will include Bach's Brandenburg Concerti Nos. 2 and 5.

name: St. Patrick's Parade of Toronto
description:
The Parade has been entertaining tourists and Torontonians since 1988! 

It is a massive celebration of Irish-Canadian culture with thousands of participants and over 100 entries. Toronto's diversity is on full display with dozens of groups marching in support of their connections to Ireland and Canada. Everyone is welcome whether you were born in Ireland, have Irish ancestors or you're just looking for a great time!

name: March Birding Walk in High Park
description:
Step into High Park as winter gives way to the first signs of spring. 

Join us for a casual beginner-friendly birding hike where we'll look for hardy winter residents and welcome the early migrants making their way back north to Toronto. All birding skill levels welcome.

Best suited for participants ages 16 and up.

name: March Break - A Puppet Show for the Whole Family
description:
March Break Shows for the Whole Family! Old Man and the River By Lynda Hill and Thomas Morgan Jones.

In a storybook world where a river sings, trees laugh and curious dragonflies come to visit, lives a grumpy little old man. Every day is the same and he likes it this way – until one day, a magical creature leaps into his world and everything changes!
Performed without words using artful tabletop puppetry and evocative music, WeeFestival's acclaimed production Old Man and the River, is a heartwarming and hilarious tale for children and their grownups! Enjoy post-show arts activities and snacks.

"It's rare to see such a tiny perfect play for such a tiny perfect price! " - Happy Parent

Recommended for children 3 years and up. Children under 3 years are free but must be on the lap of their grownup/caregiver.
